					<div id="mw-content-text" class="mw-body-content"><div class="mw-subjectpageheader">
</div><div class="mw-content-ltr mw-parser-output" lang="en" dir="ltr"><div class="shortdescription nomobile noexcerpt noprint searchaux" style="display:none">Path or line in English folklore</div>
<p>A <b>Trod</b> in the west of England is a straight line or <a href="/wiki/Fairy_path" title="Fairy path">fairy path</a> in the grass of a field with a different shade of green from the rest. Great danger was associated with using these paths when a supernatural procession was using them. <a href="/wiki/Fairy_ring" title="Fairy ring">Fairy rings</a> have certain elements in common with this phenomenon. People with <a href="/wiki/Rheumatism" title="Rheumatism">rheumatism</a> sought relief by walking along these paths; however animals were thought to actively avoid them.<sup id="cite_ref-Pennick1996132_1-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Pennick1996132-1"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>1<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup>
</p>

parsed_text: A Trod in the west of England is a straight line or fairy path in the grass of a field with a different shade of green from the rest. Great danger was associated with using these paths when a supernatural procession was using them. Fairy rings have certain elements in common with this phenomenon. People with rheumatism sought relief by walking along these paths; however animals were thought to actively avoid them.

<p>The <b>Sapienza University of Rome</b><sup id="cite_ref-7" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-7"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>7<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> (<a href="/wiki/Italian_language" title="Italian language">Italian</a>: <i lang="it">Università degli Studi di Roma "La Sapienza"</i>), also known as <b>La Sapienza</b> (The Wisdom), is a <a href="/wiki/Public_university" title="Public university">public</a> <a href="/wiki/Research_university" title="Research university">research university</a> located in <a href="/wiki/Rome" title="Rome">Rome</a>, Italy.<sup id="cite_ref-8" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-8"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>8<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> It was founded in 1303 and is one of the world's oldest universities.<sup id="cite_ref-9" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-9"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>9<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> With over 110,000 students, it is also the <a href="/wiki/List_of_largest_universities_by_enrollment" class="mw-redirect" title="List of largest universities by enrollment">largest university in Europe</a>.<sup id="cite_ref-10" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-10"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>10<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> Due to its size, funding, and numerous laboratories and libraries, Sapienza is a global major education and research centre.<sup id="cite_ref-11" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-11"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>11<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup><sup id="cite_ref-12" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-12"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>12<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> The university is located mainly in the <i>Città Universitaria</i> (University city), which covers 44 ha (110 acres) near the <a href="/wiki/Campo_Verano" title="Campo Verano">Campo Verano</a> cemetery, with different campuses, libraries and laboratories in various locations in Rome. 
</p><p>Sapienza was founded on 20 April 1303 by decree from <a href="/wiki/Pope_Boniface_VIII" title="Pope Boniface VIII">Pope Boniface VIII</a> as a <i>Studium</i> for ecclesiastical studies under more control than the free-standing universities of <a href="/wiki/University_of_Bologna" title="University of Bologna">Bologna</a> and <a href="/wiki/University_of_Padua" title="University of Padua">Padua</a>. In 1431 <a href="/wiki/Pope_Eugene_IV" title="Pope Eugene IV">Pope Eugene IV</a> completely reorganized the studium and decreed that the university should expand to include the four schools of Law, Medicine, Philosophy, in addition to the existing Theology. In the 1650s the university became known as <i>Sapienza</i>, meaning "wisdom" or "knowledge", a title it still retains.<sup id="cite_ref-13" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-13"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>13<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> After the <a href="/wiki/Capture_of_Rome" title="Capture of Rome">capture of Rome</a> by the forces of the <a href="/wiki/Kingdom_of_Italy" title="Kingdom of Italy">Kingdom of Italy</a> in 1870, <i>La Sapienza</i> rapidly expanded as the chosen main university of the capital of the newly unified state. In 1935 the new university campus, planned by <a href="/wiki/Marcello_Piacentini" title="Marcello Piacentini">Marcello Piacentini</a>, was completed.<sup id="cite_ref-:2_14-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-:2-14"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>14<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup>
</p><p>Sapienza teaches and conducts research in all pure and applied sciences and humanities. Sapienza houses 50 libraries with over 2.7 million books, most notably the Alessandrina University Library, built in 1667 by <a href="/wiki/Pope_Alexander_VII" title="Pope Alexander VII">Pope Alexander VII</a>, housing 1.5 million volumes.<sup id="cite_ref-15" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-15"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>15<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> In addition it has 19 museums, a botanical garden, and three university hospitals.<sup id="cite_ref-16" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-16"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>16<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> Sapienza's alumni includes 10 <a href="/wiki/List_of_Nobel_laureates" title="List of Nobel laureates">Nobel laureates</a>, <a href="/wiki/List_of_prime_ministers_of_Italy" title="List of prime ministers of Italy">Italian prime ministers</a>, one <a href="/wiki/List_of_popes" title="List of popes">pope</a>, <a href="/wiki/President_of_the_European_Parliament" title="President of the European Parliament">presidents of the European Parliament</a> and <a href="/wiki/European_Commissioner" class="mw-redirect" title="European Commissioner">European Commissioners</a>, as well as several notable religious figures, supreme court judges, and astronauts.<sup id="cite_ref-17" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-17"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>17<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup>
</p>

<figure typeof="mw:File/Thumb"><a href="/wiki/File:Innenhof_des_Palazzo_della_Sapienza.jpg" class="mw-file-description"><img src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e5/Innenhof_des_Palazzo_della_Sapienza.jpg/250px-Innenhof_des_Palazzo_della_Sapienza.jpg" decoding="async" width="250" height="333" class="mw-file-element" srcset="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e5/Innenhof_des_Palazzo_della_Sapienza.jpg/500px-Innenhof_des_Palazzo_della_Sapienza.jpg 2x" data-file-width="1200" data-file-height="1600"></a><figcaption><i>Palazzo della Sapienza</i>, former home of the university until 1935</figcaption></figure>
<figure class="mw-halign-left" typeof="mw:File/Thumb"><a href="/wiki/File:Sant%27Ivo_alla_Sapienza_-Rome.jpg" class="mw-file-description"><img src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/44/Sant%27Ivo_alla_Sapienza_-Rome.jpg/250px-Sant%27Ivo_alla_Sapienza_-Rome.jpg" decoding="async" width="250" height="197" class="mw-file-element" srcset="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/44/Sant%27Ivo_alla_Sapienza_-Rome.jpg/500px-Sant%27Ivo_alla_Sapienza_-Rome.jpg 2x" data-file-width="4365" data-file-height="3433"></a><figcaption>Church of <a href="/wiki/Sant%27Ivo_alla_Sapienza" title="Sant'Ivo alla Sapienza">Sant'Ivo alla Sapienza</a>, originally the chapel and seat of the university library (until 1935)</figcaption></figure>
<p>The Sapienza University of Rome was founded in 1303 with the <a href="/wiki/Papal_bull" title="Papal bull">papal bull</a> <i>In Supremae praeminentia Dignitatis</i>, issued on 20 April 1303 by <a href="/wiki/Pope_Boniface_VIII" title="Pope Boniface VIII">Pope Boniface VIII</a>, as a <i>Studium</i> for ecclesiastical studies more under his control than the universities of <a href="/wiki/University_of_Bologna" title="University of Bologna">Bologna</a> and <a href="/wiki/University_of_Padua" title="University of Padua">Padua</a>,<sup id="cite_ref-:0_18-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-:0-18"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>18<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> making it the first pontifical university.<sup id="cite_ref-:2_14-1" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-:2-14"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>14<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup>
</p><p>In 1431 <a href="/wiki/Pope_Eugene_IV" title="Pope Eugene IV">Pope Eugene IV</a> completely reorganized the studium with the bull <i>In supremae</i>, in which he granted masters and students alike the broadest possible privileges and decreed that the university should include the four schools of Law, Medicine, Philosophy and Theology. He introduced a new tax on wine to raise funds for the university; the money was used to buy a palace which later housed the <a href="/wiki/Sant%27Ivo_alla_Sapienza" title="Sant'Ivo alla Sapienza">Sant'Ivo alla Sapienza</a> church.
</p><p>However, the university's days of splendour came to an end during the <a href="/wiki/Sack_of_Rome_(1527)" title="Sack of Rome (1527)">sack of Rome</a> in 1527, when the studium was closed, some of the professors were killed and others dispersed.<sup id="cite_ref-19" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-19"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>19<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> <a href="/wiki/Pope_Paul_III" title="Pope Paul III">Pope Paul III</a> restored the university shortly after his election to the pontificate in 1534.
</p><p>In the 1650s the university became known as Sapienza, meaning wisdom, a title it retains. In 1703, with his private funds, <a href="/wiki/Pope_Clement_XI" title="Pope Clement XI">Pope Clement XI</a> purchased some land on the <a href="/wiki/Janiculum" title="Janiculum">Janiculum</a>, where he created a botanical garden, which soon became the most celebrated in Europe through the labours of the Trionfetti brothers. The first complete history of the Sapienza University was written in 1803–1806 by <a href="/wiki/Filippo_Maria_Renazzi" title="Filippo Maria Renazzi">Filippo Maria Renazzi</a>.<sup id="cite_ref-DiSimone_20-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-DiSimone-20"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>20<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup>
</p><p>University students were newly animated during the 19th-century Italian revival. In 1870, <i>La Sapienza</i> stopped being the papal university and became the university of the capital of Italy. In 1935 the new university campus, planned by <a href="/wiki/Marcello_Piacentini" title="Marcello Piacentini">Marcello Piacentini</a>, was completed.
</p><p>On 15 January 2008 the <a href="/wiki/Holy_See" title="Holy See">Vatican</a> cancelled a planned visit to La Sapienza University by <a href="/wiki/Pope_Benedict_XVI" title="Pope Benedict XVI">Pope Benedict XVI</a> who was to speak at the university ceremony launching the 2008 academic year<sup id="cite_ref-bbc_21-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-bbc-21"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>21<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> due to protests by some students and professors.<sup id="cite_ref-22" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-22"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>22<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> The title of the speech would have been 'The Truth Makes Us Good and Goodness is Truth'.<sup id="cite_ref-23" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-23"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>23<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> Some students and professors protested in reaction to a 1990 speech that Pope Benedict XVI (then Cardinal Joseph Ratzinger) gave in which he, in their opinion, endorsed the actions of the church against <a href="/wiki/Galileo" class="mw-redirect" title="Galileo">Galileo</a> in 1633.<sup id="cite_ref-:2_14-2" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-:2-14"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>14<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup><sup id="cite_ref-bbc_21-1" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-bbc-21"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>21<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup>
</p>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading2"><h2 id="Campuses">Campuses</h2><span class="mw-editsection"><span class="mw-editsection-bracket">[</span><a href="/w/index.php?title=Sapienza_University_of_Rome&action=edit&section=2" title="Edit section: Campuses"><span>edit</span></a><span class="mw-editsection-bracket">]</span></span></div>
<figure class="mw-halign-right" typeof="mw:File/Thumb"><a href="/wiki/File:RomeUniversity1938.jpg" class="mw-file-description"><img src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c7/RomeUniversity1938.jpg/250px-RomeUniversity1938.jpg" decoding="async" width="250" height="158" class="mw-file-element" srcset="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c7/RomeUniversity1938.jpg/500px-RomeUniversity1938.jpg 2x" data-file-width="1800" data-file-height="1137"></a><figcaption>The new campus of Rome University, built in 1935 by <a href="/wiki/Marcello_Piacentini" title="Marcello Piacentini">Marcello Piacentini</a>, in a 1938 picture</figcaption></figure>
<figure class="mw-halign-right" typeof="mw:File/Thumb"><a href="/wiki/File:Sapienza_entrance_(20040201351).jpg" class="mw-file-description"><img src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f4/Sapienza_entrance_%2820040201351%29.jpg/250px-Sapienza_entrance_%2820040201351%29.jpg" decoding="async" width="250" height="166" class="mw-file-element" srcset="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f4/Sapienza_entrance_%2820040201351%29.jpg/500px-Sapienza_entrance_%2820040201351%29.jpg 2x" data-file-width="4288" data-file-height="2848"></a><figcaption>Entrance of "La Sapienza" University of Rome</figcaption></figure>
<p>Sapienza University has many campuses in Rome, but its main campus is the <i>Città Universitaria</i> (University city), which covers 44 ha (110 acres) near the <a href="/wiki/Roma_Tiburtina_railway_station" title="Roma Tiburtina railway station">Roma Tiburtina Station</a>. The university has satellite campuses outside Rome, the main one of which is in <a href="/wiki/Latina,_Lazio" title="Latina, Lazio">Latina</a>.
</p><p>In 2011 a project was launched to build a campus with <a href="/wiki/Residence_halls" class="mw-redirect" title="Residence halls">residence halls</a> near <a href="/wiki/Pietralata_station" class="mw-redirect" title="Pietralata station">Pietralata station</a>, in collaboration with the <a href="/wiki/Lazio" title="Lazio">Lazio</a> region.<sup id="cite_ref-24" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-24"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>24<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> To cope with the ever-increasing number of applicants, the Rector also approved a new plan to expand the <i>Città Universitaria</i>, reallocate offices and enlarge faculties, as well as create new campuses for hosting local and foreign students.
</p><p>The Alessandrina University Library<sup id="cite_ref-:1_25-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-:1-25"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>25<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> (<i>Biblioteca Universitaria Alessandrina</i>), built in 1667 by <a href="/wiki/Pope_Alexander_VII" title="Pope Alexander VII">Pope Alexander VII</a>, is the main library housing 1.5 million volumes; it has some important collections including <i>collezione ciceroniana</i> and <i>Fondo Festa</i>.
</p>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading3"><h3 id="Points_of_interest">Points of interest</h3><span class="mw-editsection"><span class="mw-editsection-bracket">[</span><a href="/w/index.php?title=Sapienza_University_of_Rome&action=edit&section=3" title="Edit section: Points of interest"><span>edit</span></a><span class="mw-editsection-bracket">]</span></span></div>
<ul><li><a href="/wiki/Orto_Botanico_dell%27Universit%C3%A0_di_Roma_%22La_Sapienza%22" title="Orto Botanico dell'Università di Roma "La Sapienza"">Orto Botanico dell'Università di Roma "La Sapienza"</a>, a <a href="/wiki/Botanical_garden" title="Botanical garden">botanical garden</a></li>
<li><a href="/wiki/Sant%27Ivo_alla_Sapienza" title="Sant'Ivo alla Sapienza">Sant'Ivo alla Sapienza</a></li>
<li><a href="/wiki/San_Pietro_in_Vincoli" title="San Pietro in Vincoli">San Pietro in Vincoli</a>: the cloister is part of the Engineering School</li>
<li>Villa Mirafiori: a <a href="/wiki/Neo-Renaissance" class="mw-redirect" title="Neo-Renaissance">Neo-Renaissance</a> palace built during the 19th century, some rooms are decorated with fine frescoes. The Department of Philosophy is located in this building.</li></ul>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading3"><h3 id="Centro_MedioEvA">Centro MedioEvA</h3><span class="mw-editsection"><span class="mw-editsection-bracket">[</span><a href="/w/index.php?title=Sapienza_University_of_Rome&action=edit&section=4" title="Edit section: Centro MedioEvA"><span>edit</span></a><span class="mw-editsection-bracket">]</span></span></div>
<p>Sapienza University of Rome is one of the three institutions that, in 2022, contributed to the founding of the <i><a rel="nofollow" class="external text" href="https://medioeva.unisi.it/">Centro MedioEvA</a></i>. Founded by Donatella Manzoli (Sapienza University) and Elisabetta Bartoli (University of Siena), the Centre brings together a group of specialists in <a href="/wiki/Medieval_literature" title="Medieval literature">medieval literatures</a> from the <a href="/wiki/University_of_Siena" title="University of Siena">University of Siena</a>, Sapienza University, and the <a href="/wiki/University_of_Tours" title="University of Tours">University of Tours</a>. This entirely new cultural initiative aims to promote, coordinate, and carry out research activities, projects, printed publications, and digital resources on literature composed in Latin and in the vernacular languages by women during the medieval millennium (6th–15th centuries).
</p><p>Adopting a multidisciplinary and comparative approach, MedioEvA seeks to investigate women's literatures and the cultural role of women within the medieval world, challenging deeply rooted yet anachronistic stereotypes. MedioEvA also aims to explore both the objective and subjective perceptions of women across the centuries of the <a href="/wiki/Middle_Ages" title="Middle Ages">Middle Ages</a>, and—through the tools of <a href="/wiki/Philology" title="Philology">philological</a> and <a href="/wiki/Literary_criticism" title="Literary criticism">literary criticism</a>—it fosters studies, critical editions, editions of unpublished works, and translations of texts that have either never been translated or are in need of new renderings.
</p><p>MedioEvA also aspires to serve as a key platform for disseminating and promoting this little-known body of literature beyond the narrow circle of specialists, acting as a promoter of initiatives that seek to convey an updated understanding of the cultural role of medieval women and of the <a href="/wiki/Middle_Ages" title="Middle Ages">Middle Ages</a> as a whole (such as student outreach, teacher training, and participation in cultural festivals). Knowledge of women's literatures will enrich the contours of the Middle Ages, which forms the recognised foundation of our present.<sup id="cite_ref-26" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-26"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>26<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup>
</p><p>Among the scholarly works supported by the Centro MedioEvA, two notable examples are the volumes <i>Scrittrici del Medioevo. Un'antologia</i><sup id="cite_ref-27" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-27"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>27<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> and <i>Genere e generi. Scritture di donne nell'Europa medievale</i>.<sup id="cite_ref-28" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-28"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>28<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup>
</p>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading2"><h2 id="Academics">Academics</h2><span class="mw-editsection"><span class="mw-editsection-bracket">[</span><a href="/w/index.php?title=Sapienza_University_of_Rome&action=edit&section=5" title="Edit section: Academics"><span>edit</span></a><span class="mw-editsection-bracket">]</span></span></div>
<p>Since the 2011 reform, Sapienza University of Rome has eleven faculties and 65 departments. Today Sapienza, with 140,000 students and 8,000 among academic and technical and administrative staff, is the largest university in Italy. The university has significant research programmes in the fields of engineering, <a href="/wiki/Natural_sciences" class="mw-redirect" title="Natural sciences">natural sciences</a>, <a href="/wiki/Biomedical_sciences" title="Biomedical sciences">biomedical sciences</a> and <a href="/wiki/Humanities" title="Humanities">humanities</a>. It offers 10 Masters Programmes taught entirely in English.<sup class="noprint Inline-Template Template-Fact" style="white-space:nowrap;">[<i><a href="/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed" title="Wikipedia:Citation needed"><span title="This claim needs references to reliable sources. (December 2023)">citation needed</span></a></i>]</sup>
</p>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading2"><h2 id="Ranking">Ranking</h2><span class="mw-editsection"><span class="mw-editsection-bracket">[</span><a href="/w/index.php?title=Sapienza_University_of_Rome&action=edit&section=6" title="Edit section: Ranking"><span>edit</span></a><span class="mw-editsection-bracket">]</span></span></div>

</th></tr></tbody></table>
<p>In 2025, Sapienza ranked 1st among universities in Italy and Southern Europe in the <i><a href="/wiki/Academic_Ranking_of_World_Universities" title="Academic Ranking of World Universities">Academic Ranking of World Universities</a></i> (ARWU)<sup id="cite_ref-35" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-35"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>35<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup><sup id="cite_ref-36" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-36"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>36<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> and according to <a rel="nofollow" class="external text" href="https://lifestyle.everyeye.it/notizie/migliore-universita-italia-questa-svelato-ranking-cwur-2025-804557.html">CWUR</a>.
</p><p>As of the 2016 <i><a href="/wiki/Academic_Ranking_of_World_Universities" title="Academic Ranking of World Universities">Academic Ranking of World Universities</a></i> (ARWU), Sapienza is positioned within the 151–200 group of universities and among the top 3% of universities in the world.<sup id="cite_ref-37" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-37"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>37<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup><sup id="cite_ref-38" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-38"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>38<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup>
</p><p>In 2016, the Center for World University Rankings ranked the Sapienza University of Rome as the 90th in the world and the top in Italy in its <i>World University Rankings</i>.<sup id="cite_ref-39" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-39"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>39<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup>
</p><p>According to the QS Graduate Employability Ranking 2020, Sapienza places first amongst Italian universities in Alumni Outcomes thanks to the number of university graduates employed in large companies and in managerial positions.<sup id="cite_ref-40" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-40"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>40<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup>
</p><p>In 2024, Sapienza University of Rome ranked 134th in the world in <i><a href="/wiki/QS_World_University_Rankings" title="QS World University Rankings">QS World University Rankings</a></i>.<sup id="cite_ref-41" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-41"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>41<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> Sapienza is ranked 1st in the world by <i>QS World University Rankings</i> in the subject of Classics and Ancient History.<sup id="cite_ref-42" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-42"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>42<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> In the same ranking, Sapienza ranks 10th in the subject Archaeology.<sup id="cite_ref-43" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-43"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>43<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> Sapienza is ranked 36th in the subject Physics & Astronomy,<sup id="cite_ref-44" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-44"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>44<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> 39th in Arts and Humanities<sup id="cite_ref-45" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-45"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>45<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> and 70th in Psychology.<sup id="cite_ref-46" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-46"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>46<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup>
</p>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading2"><h2 id="Admission">Admission</h2><span class="mw-editsection"><span class="mw-editsection-bracket">[</span><a href="/w/index.php?title=Sapienza_University_of_Rome&action=edit&section=7" title="Edit section: Admission"><span>edit</span></a><span class="mw-editsection-bracket">]</span></span></div>
<p>To cope with the large demand for admission to the university courses, some faculties hold a series of entrance examinations. The entrance test often decides which candidates will have access to the undergraduate course. For some faculties, the entrance test is only a means through which the administration acknowledges the students' level of preparation. Students that do not pass the test can still enroll in their chosen degree courses but have to pass an additional exam during their first year.<sup class="noprint Inline-Template Template-Fact" style="white-space:nowrap;">[<i><a href="/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed" title="Wikipedia:Citation needed"><span title="This claim needs references to reliable sources. (December 2023)">citation needed</span></a></i>]</sup>
</p>

<div class="mw-heading mw-heading3"><h3 id="Selection_of_Alumni">Selection of Alumni</h3><span class="mw-editsection"><span class="mw-editsection-bracket">[</span><a href="/w/index.php?title=Sapienza_University_of_Rome&action=edit&section=12" title="Edit section: Selection of Alumni"><span>edit</span></a><span class="mw-editsection-bracket">]</span></span></div>
<p>Sapienza University can boast several illustrious professors and alumni from the past and the present. From <a href="/wiki/Nicolaus_Copernicus" title="Nicolaus Copernicus">Nicolaus Copernicus</a> to <a href="/wiki/Maria_Montessori" title="Maria Montessori">Maria Montessori</a>, from <a href="/wiki/Luigi_Pirandello" title="Luigi Pirandello">Luigi Pirandello</a> to <a href="/wiki/Tullio_De_Mauro" title="Tullio De Mauro">Tullio De Mauro</a>, from <a href="/wiki/Sergio_Mattarella" title="Sergio Mattarella">Sergio Mattarella</a> to <a href="/wiki/Mario_Draghi" title="Mario Draghi">Mario Draghi</a>. Numerous Nobel Prize winners have been professors or have graduated from Sapienza: <a href="/wiki/Guglielmo_Marconi" title="Guglielmo Marconi">Guglielmo Marconi</a>, <a href="/wiki/Enrico_Fermi" title="Enrico Fermi">Enrico Fermi</a>, <a href="/wiki/Daniel_Bovet" title="Daniel Bovet">Daniel Bovet</a>, <a href="/wiki/Emilio_Segr%C3%A8" title="Emilio Segrè">Emilio Segrè</a>, <a href="/wiki/Giulio_Natta" title="Giulio Natta">Giulio Natta</a>, <a href="/wiki/Carlo_Rubbia" title="Carlo Rubbia">Carlo Rubbia</a>, <a href="/wiki/Franco_Modigliani" title="Franco Modigliani">Franco Modigliani</a>.
</p>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading4"><h4 id="Nobel_Prize_Winners">Nobel Prize Winners</h4><span class="mw-editsection"><span class="mw-editsection-bracket">[</span><a href="/w/index.php?title=Sapienza_University_of_Rome&action=edit&section=13" title="Edit section: Nobel Prize Winners"><span>edit</span></a><span class="mw-editsection-bracket">]</span></span></div>
<ul><li><b><a href="/wiki/Guglielmo_Marconi" title="Guglielmo Marconi">Guglielmo Marconi</a></b> – 1909 Nobel Prize in Physics; taught Electro-magnetic Waves - Physics at Sapienza 1935–1937.</li>
<li><b><a href="/wiki/Enrico_Fermi" title="Enrico Fermi">Enrico Fermi</a></b> – 1938 Nobel Prize in Physics; Chair of Theoretical Physics at Sapienza 1926–1938.</li>
<li><b><a href="/wiki/Daniel_Bovet" title="Daniel Bovet">Daniel Bovet</a></b> – 1957 Nobel Prize in Medicine and Physiology, Chair of Psychobiology at Sapienza 1971–1982.</li>
<li><b><a href="/wiki/Emilio_Segr%C3%A8" title="Emilio Segrè">Emilio Segrè</a></b> – 1959 Nobel Prize in Physics; Chair of Physics at Sapienza 1928–1935.</li>
<li><b><a href="/wiki/Giulio_Natta" title="Giulio Natta">Giulio Natta</a></b> – 1963 Nobel Prize in Chemistry; Chair of Physical Chemistry at Sapienza 1935–1937.</li>
<li><b><a href="/wiki/Carlo_Rubbia" title="Carlo Rubbia">Carlo Rubbia</a></b> – 1984 Nobel Prize in Physics; Assistant Professor of Physics 1959–1960.</li>
<li><b><a href="/wiki/Franco_Modigliani" title="Franco Modigliani">Franco Modigliani</a></b> – 1985 Nobel Prize in Economics; graduated in Law in 1939.</li>
<li><b><a href="/wiki/Barry_C._Barish" class="mw-redirect" title="Barry C. Barish">Barry C. Barish</a></b> – 2017 Nobel Prize in Physics; Fermi Chair of Physics at Sapienza 2019.</li>
<li><b><a href="/wiki/Giorgio_Parisi" title="Giorgio Parisi">Giorgio Parisi</a></b> – 2021 Nobel Prize in Physics; Chair of Theoretical Physics 1992–2018.</li>
<li><b><a href="/wiki/Serge_Haroche" title="Serge Haroche">Serge Haroche</a></b> – 2012 Nobel Prize in Physics; Fermi Chair of Physics at Sapienza 2022.</li></ul>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading4"><h4 id="Politics,_Diplomats_&_Administrators"><span id="Politics.2C_Diplomats_.26_Administrators"></span>Politics, Diplomats & Administrators</h4><span class="mw-editsection"><span class="mw-editsection-bracket">[</span><a href="/w/index.php?title=Sapienza_University_of_Rome&action=edit&section=14" title="Edit section: Politics, Diplomats & Administrators"><span>edit</span></a><span class="mw-editsection-bracket">]</span></span></div>
<ul><li><b><a href="/wiki/Giulio_Andreotti" title="Giulio Andreotti">Giulio Andreotti</a></b> (1919–2013), politician.</li>
<li><b><a href="/wiki/Laura_Boldrini" title="Laura Boldrini">Laura Boldrini</a></b>, politician.</li>

<div class="mw-heading mw-heading3"><h3 id="Faculty_and_staff">Faculty and staff</h3><span class="mw-editsection"><span class="mw-editsection-bracket">[</span><a href="/w/index.php?title=Sapienza_University_of_Rome&action=edit&section=22" title="Edit section: Faculty and staff"><span>edit</span></a><span class="mw-editsection-bracket">]</span></span></div>
<p>Among the prominent scholars who have taught at the Sapienza University of Rome are architects <a href="/wiki/Ernesto_Basile" title="Ernesto Basile">Ernesto Basile</a> and <a href="/wiki/Bruno_Zevi" title="Bruno Zevi">Bruno Zevi</a>; chemist <a href="/wiki/Emanuele_Patern%C3%B2" title="Emanuele Paternò">Emanuele Paternò</a>; jurists <a href="/wiki/Antonio_Salandra" title="Antonio Salandra">Antonio Salandra</a>, <a href="/wiki/Sabino_Cassese" title="Sabino Cassese">Sabino Cassese</a> and <a href="/wiki/Giuliano_Amato" title="Giuliano Amato">Giuliano Amato</a>; mathematician <a href="/wiki/Vito_Volterra" title="Vito Volterra">Vito Volterra</a>; pharmacologist and Nobel Laureate in Physiology or Medicine <a href="/wiki/Daniel_Bovet" title="Daniel Bovet">Daniel Bovet</a>; chemist and Nobel Laureate <a href="/wiki/Giulio_Natta" title="Giulio Natta">Giulio Natta</a>; philosophers <a href="/wiki/Luigi_Ferri" title="Luigi Ferri">Luigi Ferri</a> and <a href="/wiki/Augusto_Del_Noce" title="Augusto Del Noce">Augusto Del Noce</a>; physicist and Nobel Laureate in Physics <a href="/wiki/Enrico_Fermi" title="Enrico Fermi">Enrico Fermi</a>; political scientist <a href="/wiki/Roberto_Forges_Davanzati" title="Roberto Forges Davanzati">Roberto Forges Davanzati</a>.

<div class="rt-overlay"></div><div id="mw-teleport-target" class="vector-body"></div><a accesskey="v" href="https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sapienza_University_of_Rome?action=edit" class="oo-ui-element-hidden"></a></body></html>
parsed_text:
The Sapienza University of Rome ( Italian: Università degli Studi di Roma "La Sapienza" ), also known as La Sapienza (The Wisdom), is a public research university located in Rome, Italy. It was founded in 1303 and is one of the world's oldest universities. With over 110,000 students, it is also the largest university in Europe. Due to its size, funding, and numerous laboratories and libraries, Sapienza is a global major education and research centre. The university is located mainly in the Città Universitaria (University city), which covers 44 ha (110 acres) near the Campo Verano cemetery, with different campuses, libraries and laboratories in various locations in Rome.

Sapienza was founded on 20 April 1303 by decree from Pope Boniface VIII as a Studium for ecclesiastical studies under more control than the free-standing universities of Bologna and Padua. In 1431 Pope Eugene IV completely reorganized the studium and decreed that the university should expand to include the four schools of Law, Medicine, Philosophy, in addition to the existing Theology. In the 1650s the university became known as Sapienza, meaning "wisdom" or "knowledge", a title it still retains. After the capture of Rome by the forces of the Kingdom of Italy in 1870, La Sapienza rapidly expanded as the chosen main university of the capital of the newly unified state. In 1935 the new university campus, planned by Marcello Piacentini, was completed.

Sapienza teaches and conducts research in all pure and applied sciences and humanities. Sapienza houses 50 libraries with over 2.7 million books, most notably the Alessandrina University Library, built in 1667 by Pope Alexander VII, housing 1.5 million volumes. In addition it has 19 museums, a botanical garden, and three university hospitals. Sapienza's alumni includes 10 Nobel laureates, Italian prime ministers, one pope, presidents of the European Parliament and European Commissioners, as well as several notable religious figures, supreme court judges, and astronauts.

## History

The Sapienza University of Rome was founded in 1303 with the papal bull In Supremae praeminentia Dignitatis, issued on 20 April 1303 by Pope Boniface VIII, as a Studium for ecclesiastical studies more under his control than the universities of Bologna and Padua, making it the first pontifical university.

In 1431 Pope Eugene IV completely reorganized the studium with the bull In supremae, in which he granted masters and students alike the broadest possible privileges and decreed that the university should include the four schools of Law, Medicine, Philosophy and Theology. He introduced a new tax on wine to raise funds for the university; the money was used to buy a palace which later housed the Sant'Ivo alla Sapienza church.

However, the university's days of splendour came to an end during the sack of Rome in 1527, when the studium was closed, some of the professors were killed and others dispersed. Pope Paul III restored the university shortly after his election to the pontificate in 1534.

In the 1650s the university became known as Sapienza, meaning wisdom, a title it retains. In 1703, with his private funds, Pope Clement XI purchased some land on the Janiculum, where he created a botanical garden, which soon became the most celebrated in Europe through the labours of the Trionfetti brothers. The first complete history of the Sapienza University was written in 1803–1806 by Filippo Maria Renazzi.

University students were newly animated during the 19th-century Italian revival. In 1870, La Sapienza stopped being the papal university and became the university of the capital of Italy. In 1935 the new university campus, planned by Marcello Piacentini, was completed.

On 15 January 2008 the Vatican cancelled a planned visit to La Sapienza University by Pope Benedict XVI who was to speak at the university ceremony launching the 2008 academic year due to protests by some students and professors. The title of the speech would have been 'The Truth Makes Us Good and Goodness is Truth'. Some students and professors protested in reaction to a 1990 speech that Pope Benedict XVI (then Cardinal Joseph Ratzinger) gave in which he, in their opinion, endorsed the actions of the church against Galileo in 1633.

## Campuses

Sapienza University has many campuses in Rome, but its main campus is the Città Universitaria (University city), which covers 44 ha (110 acres) near the Roma Tiburtina Station. The university has satellite campuses outside Rome, the main one of which is in Latina.

In 2011 a project was launched to build a campus with residence halls near Pietralata station, in collaboration with the Lazio region. To cope with the ever-increasing number of applicants, the Rector also approved a new plan to expand the Città Universitaria, reallocate offices and enlarge faculties, as well as create new campuses for hosting local and foreign students.

The Alessandrina University Library ( Biblioteca Universitaria Alessandrina ), built in 1667 by Pope Alexander VII, is the main library housing 1.5 million volumes; it has some important collections including collezione ciceroniana and Fondo Festa.

### Points of interest

- Orto Botanico dell'Università di Roma "La Sapienza" , a botanical garden
- Sant'Ivo alla Sapienza
- San Pietro in Vincoli : the cloister is part of the Engineering School
- Villa Mirafiori: a Neo-Renaissance palace built during the 19th century, some rooms are decorated with fine frescoes. The Department of Philosophy is located in this building.

### Centro MedioEvA

Sapienza University of Rome is one of the three institutions that, in 2022, contributed to the founding of the Centro MedioEvA. Founded by Donatella Manzoli (Sapienza University) and Elisabetta Bartoli (University of Siena), the Centre brings together a group of specialists in medieval literatures from the University of Siena, Sapienza University, and the University of Tours. This entirely new cultural initiative aims to promote, coordinate, and carry out research activities, projects, printed publications, and digital resources on literature composed in Latin and in the vernacular languages by women during the medieval millennium (6th–15th centuries).

Adopting a multidisciplinary and comparative approach, MedioEvA seeks to investigate women's literatures and the cultural role of women within the medieval world, challenging deeply rooted yet anachronistic stereotypes. MedioEvA also aims to explore both the objective and subjective perceptions of women across the centuries of the Middle Ages, and—through the tools of philological and literary criticism —it fosters studies, critical editions, editions of unpublished works, and translations of texts that have either never been translated or are in need of new renderings.

MedioEvA also aspires to serve as a key platform for disseminating and promoting this little-known body of literature beyond the narrow circle of specialists, acting as a promoter of initiatives that seek to convey an updated understanding of the cultural role of medieval women and of the Middle Ages as a whole (such as student outreach, teacher training, and participation in cultural festivals). Knowledge of women's literatures will enrich the contours of the Middle Ages, which forms the recognised foundation of our present.

Among the scholarly works supported by the Centro MedioEvA, two notable examples are the volumes Scrittrici del Medioevo. Un'antologia and Genere e generi. Scritture di donne nell'Europa medievale.

## Academics

Since the 2011 reform, Sapienza University of Rome has eleven faculties and 65 departments. Today Sapienza, with 140,000 students and 8,000 among academic and technical and administrative staff, is the largest university in Italy. The university has significant research programmes in the fields of engineering, natural sciences, biomedical sciences and humanities. It offers 10 Masters Programmes taught entirely in English. [ citation needed ]

## Ranking

In 2025, Sapienza ranked 1st among universities in Italy and Southern Europe in the Academic Ranking of World Universities (ARWU) and according to CWUR.

As of the 2016 Academic Ranking of World Universities (ARWU), Sapienza is positioned within the 151–200 group of universities and among the top 3% of universities in the world.

In 2016, the Center for World University Rankings ranked the Sapienza University of Rome as the 90th in the world and the top in Italy in its World University Rankings.

According to the QS Graduate Employability Ranking 2020, Sapienza places first amongst Italian universities in Alumni Outcomes thanks to the number of university graduates employed in large companies and in managerial positions.

In 2024, Sapienza University of Rome ranked 134th in the world in QS World University Rankings. Sapienza is ranked 1st in the world by QS World University Rankings in the subject of Classics and Ancient History. In the same ranking, Sapienza ranks 10th in the subject Archaeology. Sapienza is ranked 36th in the subject Physics & Astronomy, 39th in Arts and Humanities and 70th in Psychology.

## Admission

To cope with the large demand for admission to the university courses, some faculties hold a series of entrance examinations. The entrance test often decides which candidates will have access to the undergraduate course. For some faculties, the entrance test is only a means through which the administration acknowledges the students' level of preparation. Students that do not pass the test can still enroll in their chosen degree courses but have to pass an additional exam during their first year. [ citation needed ]

### Selection of Alumni

Sapienza University can boast several illustrious professors and alumni from the past and the present. From Nicolaus Copernicus to Maria Montessori, from Luigi Pirandello to Tullio De Mauro, from Sergio Mattarella to Mario Draghi. Numerous Nobel Prize winners have been professors or have graduated from Sapienza: Guglielmo Marconi, Enrico Fermi, Daniel Bovet, Emilio Segrè, Giulio Natta, Carlo Rubbia, Franco Modigliani.

- Guglielmo Marconi – 1909 Nobel Prize in Physics; taught Electro-magnetic Waves - Physics at Sapienza 1935–1937.
- Enrico Fermi – 1938 Nobel Prize in Physics; Chair of Theoretical Physics at Sapienza 1926–1938.
- Daniel Bovet – 1957 Nobel Prize in Medicine and Physiology, Chair of Psychobiology at Sapienza 1971–1982.
- Emilio Segrè – 1959 Nobel Prize in Physics; Chair of Physics at Sapienza 1928–1935.
- Giulio Natta – 1963 Nobel Prize in Chemistry; Chair of Physical Chemistry at Sapienza 1935–1937.
- Carlo Rubbia – 1984 Nobel Prize in Physics; Assistant Professor of Physics 1959–1960.
- Franco Modigliani – 1985 Nobel Prize in Economics; graduated in Law in 1939.

- Giorgio Parisi – 2021 Nobel Prize in Physics; Chair of Theoretical Physics 1992–2018.
- Serge Haroche – 2012 Nobel Prize in Physics; Fermi Chair of Physics at Sapienza 2022.

					<div id="mw-content-text" class="mw-body-content"><div class="mw-subjectpageheader">
</div><div class="mw-content-ltr mw-parser-output" lang="en" dir="ltr"><div class="shortdescription nomobile noexcerpt noprint searchaux" style="display:none">Reconstructed ancestor of the Romance languages</div>
<style data-mw-deduplicate="TemplateStyles:r1126788409">.mw-parser-output .plainlist ol,.mw-parser-output .plainlist ul{line-height:inherit;list-style:none;margin:0;padding:0}.mw-parser-output .plainlist ol li,.mw-parser-output .plainlist ul li{margin-bottom:0}</style><style data-mw-deduplicate="TemplateStyles:r1316064257">.mw-parser-output .infobox-subbox{padding:0;border:none;margin:-3px;width:auto;min-width:100%;font-size:100%;clear:none;float:none;background-color:transparent;color:inherit}.mw-parser-output .infobox-3cols-child{margin:-3px}.mw-parser-output .infobox .navbar{font-size:100%}@media screen{html.skin-theme-clientpref-night .mw-parser-output .infobox-full-data:not(.notheme)>div:not(.notheme)[style]{background:#1f1f23!important;color:#f8f9fa}}@media screen and (prefers-color-scheme:dark){html.skin-theme-clientpref-os .mw-parser-output .infobox-full-data:not(.notheme)>div:not(.notheme)[style]{background:#1f1f23!important;color:#f8f9fa}}@media(min-width:640px){body.skin--responsive .mw-parser-output .infobox-table{display:table!important}body.skin--responsive .mw-parser-output .infobox-table>caption{display:table-caption!important}body.skin--responsive .mw-parser-output .infobox-table>tbody{display:table-row-group}body.skin--responsive .mw-parser-output .infobox-table th,body.skin--responsive .mw-parser-output .infobox-table td{padding-left:inherit;padding-right:inherit}}</style><table class="infobox" style="width:22em;"><tbody><tr><th colspan="2" class="infobox-above" style="background-color:  #c9ffd9; color:inherit;">Proto-Romance</th></tr><tr><th scope="row" class="infobox-label">Reconstruction of</th><td class="infobox-data"><a href="/wiki/Romance_languages" title="Romance languages">Romance languages</a></td></tr><tr><th scope="row" class="infobox-label">Region</th><td class="infobox-data"><a href="/wiki/Roman_Empire" title="Roman Empire">Roman Empire</a></td></tr><tr><th scope="row" class="infobox-label"><div style="display: inline-block; line-height: 1.2em; padding: .1em 0;">Reconstructed<br>ancestors</div></th><td class="infobox-data"><div style="text-align:left;"><a href="/wiki/Proto-Indo-European_language" title="Proto-Indo-European language">Proto-Indo-European</a>
<ul style="line-height:100%; margin-left:1.35em; padding-left:0"><li><a href="/wiki/Proto-Italic_language" title="Proto-Italic language">Proto-Italic</a>

</li></ul>
</div></td></tr><tr class="plainlist"><th scope="row" class="infobox-label">Lower-order reconstructions</th><td class="infobox-data">
<ul><li><a href="/wiki/Common_Romanian" title="Common Romanian">Common Romanian</a></li>
<li>Proto-Italo-Western Romance</li></ul>
</td></tr></tbody></table>
<p><b>Proto-Romance</b> is the result of applying the <a href="/wiki/Comparative_method" title="Comparative method">comparative method</a> to <a href="/wiki/Linguistic_reconstruction" title="Linguistic reconstruction">reconstruct</a> the latest common ancestor of the <a href="/wiki/Romance_languages" title="Romance languages">Romance languages</a>. The closest real-life counterpart to Proto-Romance would have been a colloquial variety of <a href="/wiki/Late_Latin" title="Late Latin">Late Latin</a> sometimes referred to as <a href="/wiki/Vulgar_Latin" title="Vulgar Latin">Vulgar Latin</a>.
</p>
<meta property="mw:PageProp/toc">
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading2"><h2 id="Phonology">Phonology</h2><span class="mw-editsection"><span class="mw-editsection-bracket">[</span><a href="/w/index.php?title=Proto-Romance_language&action=edit&section=1" title="Edit section: Phonology"><span>edit</span></a><span class="mw-editsection-bracket">]</span></span></div>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading3"><h3 id="Vowels">Vowels</h3><span class="mw-editsection"><span class="mw-editsection-bracket">[</span><a href="/w/index.php?title=Proto-Romance_language&action=edit&section=2" title="Edit section: Vowels"><span>edit</span></a><span class="mw-editsection-bracket">]</span></span></div>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading4"><h4 id="Monophthongs">Monophthongs</h4><span class="mw-editsection"><span class="mw-editsection-bracket">[</span><a href="/w/index.php?title=Proto-Romance_language&action=edit&section=3" title="Edit section: Monophthongs"><span>edit</span></a><span class="mw-editsection-bracket">]</span></span></div>
<table class="wikitable" style="text-align: center;">
<tbody><tr>
<th>
</th>
<th><a href="/wiki/Front_vowel" title="Front vowel">Front</a>
</th>
<th><a href="/wiki/Central_vowel" title="Central vowel">Central</a>
</th>

</th></tr>
<tr>
<th><a href="/wiki/Close_vowel" title="Close vowel">Close</a>
</th>
<td><span class="IPA" lang="und-Latn-fonipa"><a href="/wiki/Close_front_unrounded_vowel" title="Close front unrounded vowel">i</a></span>
</td>
<td>
</td>
<td><span class="IPA" lang="und-Latn-fonipa"><a href="/wiki/Close_back_rounded_vowel" title="Close back rounded vowel">u</a></span>
</td></tr>
<tr>
<th><a href="/wiki/Near-close_vowel" title="Near-close vowel">Near-close</a>
</th>
<td><span class="IPA" lang="und-Latn-fonipa"><a href="/wiki/Near-close_near-front_unrounded_vowel" title="Near-close near-front unrounded vowel">ɪ</a></span>
</td>
<td>
</td>
<td><span class="IPA" lang="und-Latn-fonipa"><a href="/wiki/Near-close_near-back_rounded_vowel" title="Near-close near-back rounded vowel">ʊ</a></span>
</td></tr>
<tr>
<th><a href="/wiki/Close-mid_vowel" title="Close-mid vowel">Close-mid</a>
</th>
<td><span class="IPA" lang="und-Latn-fonipa"><a href="/wiki/Close-mid_front_unrounded_vowel" title="Close-mid front unrounded vowel">e</a></span>
</td>
<td>
</td>
<td><span class="IPA" lang="und-Latn-fonipa"><a href="/wiki/Close-mid_back_rounded_vowel" title="Close-mid back rounded vowel">o</a></span>
</td></tr>

<th><a href="/wiki/Open-mid_vowel" title="Open-mid vowel">Open-mid</a>
</th>
<td><span class="IPA" lang="und-Latn-fonipa"><a href="/wiki/Open-mid_front_unrounded_vowel" title="Open-mid front unrounded vowel">ɛ</a></span>
</td>
<td>
</td>
<td><span class="IPA" lang="und-Latn-fonipa"><a href="/wiki/Open-mid_back_rounded_vowel" title="Open-mid back rounded vowel">ɔ</a></span>
</td></tr>
<tr>
<th><a href="/wiki/Open_vowel" title="Open vowel">Open</a>
</th>
<td>
</td>
<td><span class="IPA" lang="und-Latn-fonipa"><a href="/wiki/Open_central_unrounded_vowel" title="Open central unrounded vowel">a</a></span>
</td>

<div class="mw-heading mw-heading4"><h4 id="Diphthong">Diphthong</h4><span class="mw-editsection"><span class="mw-editsection-bracket">[</span><a href="/w/index.php?title=Proto-Romance_language&action=edit&section=4" title="Edit section: Diphthong"><span>edit</span></a><span class="mw-editsection-bracket">]</span></span></div>
<p><span class="IPA nowrap" lang="und-Latn-fonipa" title="Representation in the International Phonetic Alphabet (IPA)">/au̯/</span> appears to be the only phonemic diphthong that can be reconstructed.<sup id="cite_ref-1" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-1"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>1<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup>
</p>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading4"><h4 id="Phonetics">Phonetics</h4><span class="mw-editsection"><span class="mw-editsection-bracket">[</span><a href="/w/index.php?title=Proto-Romance_language&action=edit&section=5" title="Edit section: Phonetics"><span>edit</span></a><span class="mw-editsection-bracket">]</span></span></div>
<ul><li>Vowels were lengthened in stressed open syllables.<sup id="cite_ref-2" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-2"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>2<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup></li>
<li>Stressed <span class="IPA nowrap" lang="und-Latn-fonipa" title="Representation in the International Phonetic Alphabet (IPA)">/ɛ<span class="wrap"> </span>ɔ/</span> may have yielded incipient <a href="/wiki/Diphthong" title="Diphthong">diphthongs</a> like <span class="IPA nowrap" lang="und-Latn-fonipa" title="Representation in the International Phonetic Alphabet (IPA)">[e͡ɛ<span class="wrap"> </span>o͡ɔ]</span> in <a href="/wiki/Metaphony_(Romance_languages)" title="Metaphony (Romance languages)">metaphonic</a> conditions.<sup id="cite_ref-3" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-3"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>3<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup><sup id="cite_ref-4" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-4"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>i<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup>
<ul><li>Metaphony, if it can be projected back to Proto-Romance, may have initially been limited to open syllables. That is, it would have targeted allophonically lengthened <span class="IPA nowrap" lang="und-Latn-fonipa" title="Representation in the International Phonetic Alphabet (IPA)">/ɛ<span class="wrap"> </span>ɔ/</span>.<sup id="cite_ref-5" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-5"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>4<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup></li></ul></li></ul>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading4"><h4 id="Constraints">Constraints</h4><span class="mw-editsection"><span class="mw-editsection-bracket">[</span><a href="/w/index.php?title=Proto-Romance_language&action=edit&section=6" title="Edit section: Constraints"><span>edit</span></a><span class="mw-editsection-bracket">]</span></span></div>
<ul><li><span class="IPA nowrap" lang="und-Latn-fonipa" title="Representation in the International Phonetic Alphabet (IPA)">/ɛ<span class="wrap"> </span>ɔ/</span> did not occur in unstressed position.<sup id="cite_ref-:1_6-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-:1-6"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>5<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup></li>
<li><span class="IPA nowrap" lang="und-Latn-fonipa" title="Representation in the International Phonetic Alphabet (IPA)">/i<span class="wrap"> </span>u/</span> did not occur in the second syllable of words with the structure <a href="/wiki/Syllable#Transcription" title="Syllable">ˌσσˈσσ</a>.<sup id="cite_ref-7" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-7"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>6<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup><sup id="cite_ref-8" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-8"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>ii<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup></li></ul>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading3"><h3 id="Consonants">Consonants</h3><span class="mw-editsection"><span class="mw-editsection-bracket">[</span><a href="/w/index.php?title=Proto-Romance_language&action=edit&section=7" title="Edit section: Consonants"><span>edit</span></a><span class="mw-editsection-bracket">]</span></span></div>

<div class="mw-heading mw-heading4"><h4 id="Palatalized_consonants">Palatalized consonants</h4><span class="mw-editsection"><span class="mw-editsection-bracket">[</span><a href="/w/index.php?title=Proto-Romance_language&action=edit&section=8" title="Edit section: Palatalized consonants"><span>edit</span></a><span class="mw-editsection-bracket">]</span></span></div>
<style data-mw-deduplicate="TemplateStyles:r1320445320">.mw-parser-output .hatnote{font-style:italic}.mw-parser-output div.hatnote{padding-left:1.6em;margin-bottom:0.5em}.mw-parser-output .hatnote i{font-style:normal}.mw-parser-output .hatnote+link+.hatnote{margin-top:-0.5em}@media print{body.ns-0 .mw-parser-output .hatnote{display:none!important}}</style><div role="note" class="hatnote navigation-not-searchable">See also: <a href="/wiki/Palatalization_in_the_Romance_languages#/Cj/" title="Palatalization in the Romance languages">Palatalization in the Romance languages § /Cj/</a></div>
<ul><li>There is scholarly disagreement over whether palatalization was phonemic in Proto-Romance.<sup id="cite_ref-9" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-9"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>7<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup><sup id="cite_ref-10" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-10"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>iii<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup></li>
<li>Palatalized consonants tended to <a href="/wiki/Gemination" title="Gemination">geminate</a> between vowels. The extent of this varied by consonant.<sup id="cite_ref-11" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-11"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>8<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup><i><sup id="cite_ref-12" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-12"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>iv<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup></i></li>
<li><span class="IPA nowrap" lang="und-Latn-fonipa" title="Representation in the International Phonetic Alphabet (IPA)">/tʲ/</span> would have been an <a href="/wiki/Voiceless_alveolar_affricate" title="Voiceless alveolar affricate">affricate</a> like <span class="IPA nowrap" lang="und-Latn-fonipa" title="Representation in the International Phonetic Alphabet (IPA)">[t͡sʲ]</span><sup id="cite_ref-13" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-13"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>9<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> or <span class="IPA nowrap" lang="und-Latn-fonipa" title="Representation in the International Phonetic Alphabet (IPA)">[t͡zʲ]</span>.<sup id="cite_ref-14" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-14"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>10<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup></li></ul>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading4"><h4 id="Phonetics_2">Phonetics</h4><span class="mw-editsection"><span class="mw-editsection-bracket">[</span><a href="/w/index.php?title=Proto-Romance_language&action=edit&section=9" title="Edit section: Phonetics"><span>edit</span></a><span class="mw-editsection-bracket">]</span></span></div>
<ul><li><span class="IPA nowrap" lang="und-Latn-fonipa" title="Representation in the International Phonetic Alphabet (IPA)">/sC/</span> in word-initial position was assigned a <a href="/wiki/Prothesis_(linguistics)" title="Prothesis (linguistics)">prop-vowel</a> [ɪ], as in <span class="IPA nowrap" lang="und-Latn-fonipa" title="Representation in the International Phonetic Alphabet (IPA)">/ˈstare/</span> <span class="IPA nowrap" lang="und-Latn-fonipa" title="Representation in the International Phonetic Alphabet (IPA)">[ɪsˈtaːɾe]</span>.<sup id="cite_ref-15" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-15"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>11<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup><i><sup id="cite_ref-16" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-16"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>v<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup></i></li>
<li><span class="IPA nowrap" lang="und-Latn-fonipa" title="Representation in the International Phonetic Alphabet (IPA)">/ɡn/</span> was likely <span class="IPA nowrap" lang="und-Latn-fonipa" title="Representation in the International Phonetic Alphabet (IPA)">[ɣn]</span> at first, with later developments varying by region.<sup id="cite_ref-17" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-17"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>12<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup><i><sup id="cite_ref-18" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-18"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>vi<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup></i></li>
<li><span class="IPA nowrap" lang="und-Latn-fonipa" title="Representation in the International Phonetic Alphabet (IPA)">/d<span class="wrap"> </span>ɡ/</span> might have been fricatives or approximants between vowels.<sup id="cite_ref-19" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-19"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>13<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup></li>
<li><span class="IPA nowrap" lang="und-Latn-fonipa" title="Representation in the International Phonetic Alphabet (IPA)">/ll/</span> might have been <a href="/wiki/Voiced_retroflex_lateral_approximant" title="Voiced retroflex lateral approximant">retroflex</a>.<sup id="cite_ref-20" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-20"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>14<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup><i><sup id="cite_ref-21" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-21"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>vii<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup></i></li>
<li><span class="IPA nowrap" lang="und-Latn-fonipa" title="Representation in the International Phonetic Alphabet (IPA)">/f/</span> might have been <a href="/wiki/Voiceless_bilabial_fricative" title="Voiceless bilabial fricative">bilabial</a>.<sup id="cite_ref-22" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-22"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>15<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup></li></ul>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading4"><h4 id="Constraints_2">Constraints</h4><span class="mw-editsection"><span class="mw-editsection-bracket">[</span><a href="/w/index.php?title=Proto-Romance_language&action=edit&section=10" title="Edit section: Constraints"><span>edit</span></a><span class="mw-editsection-bracket">]</span></span></div>
<ul><li><span class="IPA nowrap" lang="und-Latn-fonipa" title="Representation in the International Phonetic Alphabet (IPA)">/b/</span> did not occur in intervocalic position.<sup id="cite_ref-23" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-23"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>16<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup><sup id="cite_ref-24" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-24"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>viii<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup></li></ul>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading2"><h2 id="Morphology">Morphology</h2><span class="mw-editsection"><span class="mw-editsection-bracket">[</span><a href="/w/index.php?title=Proto-Romance_language&action=edit&section=11" title="Edit section: Morphology"><span>edit</span></a><span class="mw-editsection-bracket">]</span></span></div>
<p>The forms below are spelt as they are in the cited sources, either in Latin style or in phonetic notation. The latter may not always agree with the phonology given above.
</p>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading3"><h3 id="Nouns">Nouns</h3><span class="mw-editsection"><span class="mw-editsection-bracket">[</span><a href="/w/index.php?title=Proto-Romance_language&action=edit&section=12" title="Edit section: Nouns"><span>edit</span></a><span class="mw-editsection-bracket">]</span></span></div>
<p>Nouns are reconstructed as having three cases: a <a href="/wiki/Nominative_case" title="Nominative case">nominative</a>, an <a href="/wiki/Accusative_case" title="Accusative case">accusative</a>, and a <a href="/wiki/Genitive_case" title="Genitive case">genitive</a>-<a href="/wiki/Dative_case" title="Dative case">dative</a>:<sup id="cite_ref-25" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-25"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>17<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup><i><sup id="cite_ref-26" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-26"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>ix<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup></i>
</p>

<p>Some nouns of the –C type had inflections with alternating stress or syllable count:<sup id="cite_ref-27" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-27"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>18<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup>

<p>There were also ‘neuter’ nouns. In the singular they would have been treated as masculine and in the plural as feminine, often with a collective sense.<sup id="cite_ref-28" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-28"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>19<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup>

<div class="mw-heading mw-heading4"><h4 id="Comparative">Comparative</h4><span class="mw-editsection"><span class="mw-editsection-bracket">[</span><a href="/w/index.php?title=Proto-Romance_language&action=edit&section=15" title="Edit section: Comparative"><span>edit</span></a><span class="mw-editsection-bracket">]</span></span></div>
<p>For the most part, the typical way to form a comparative would have been to add <i>magis</i> or <i>plus</i> (‘more’) to a positive adjective. A few words can be reconstructed as having a comparative ending <i>-ior</i>, which would have been inflected as follows:<i><sup id="cite_ref-29" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-29"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>20<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup></i>
</p>

<div class="mw-heading mw-heading4"><h4 id="Superlative">Superlative</h4><span class="mw-editsection"><span class="mw-editsection-bracket">[</span><a href="/w/index.php?title=Proto-Romance_language&action=edit&section=16" title="Edit section: Superlative"><span>edit</span></a><span class="mw-editsection-bracket">]</span></span></div>
<p>Superlatives would have been formed by adding definite articles to comparatives.<sup id="cite_ref-30" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-30"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>21<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup>
</p>

<li id="cite_note-26"><span class="mw-cite-backlink"><b><a href="#cite_ref-26" aria-label="Jump up" title="Jump up">^</a></b></span> <span class="reference-text"><a href="#CITEREFde_DardelGaeng1992">de Dardel & Gaeng (1992</a>:104) differ from Lausberg on the following points: 1) They believe that the genitive-dative case was limited to animate nouns. 2) They reconstruct a universal gen-dat. plural ending -orum. 3) They reconstruct, for class -a type nouns, a nominative plural -ae, albeit one in competition with -as according to <a href="#CITEREFde_DardelWüest1993">de Dardel & Wüest (1993</a>:57). parsed_text:
Proto-Romance is the result of applying the comparative method to reconstruct the latest common ancestor of the Romance languages. The closest real-life counterpart to Proto-Romance would have been a colloquial variety of Late Latin sometimes referred to as Vulgar Latin.

## Phonology

### Vowels

|  | Front | Central | Back |
|---|---|---|---|
| Close | i |  | u |
| Near-close | ɪ |  | ʊ |
| Close-mid | e |  | o |
| Open-mid | ɛ |  | ɔ |
| Open |  | a |  |

/au̯/ appears to be the only phonemic diphthong that can be reconstructed.

- Vowels were lengthened in stressed open syllables.
- Stressed /ɛ ɔ/ may have yielded incipient diphthongs like [e͡ɛ o͡ɔ] in metaphonic conditions. Metaphony, if it can be projected back to Proto-Romance, may have initially been limited to open syllables. That is, it would have targeted allophonically lengthened /ɛ ɔ/ .

- Metaphony, if it can be projected back to Proto-Romance, may have initially been limited to open syllables. That is, it would have targeted allophonically lengthened /ɛ ɔ/ .

- /ɛ ɔ/ did not occur in unstressed position.
- /i u/ did not occur in the second syllable of words with the structure ˌσσˈσσ .

### Consonants

|  | Labial | Alveolar | Palatal | Velar |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Nasal | m | mʲ | n | nʲ |  |  |
| Plosive | voiceless | p | pʲ | t | tʲ |  | k | kʲ |
| voiced | b | bʲ | d | dʲ |  | ɡ | ɡʲ |
| Fricative | voiceless | f | fʲ | s | sʲ |  |  |
| voiced | β | βʲ |  |  |  |
| Approximant |  |  | ( j ) | ( w ) |
| Lateral Approximant |  | l | lʲ |  |  |
| Trill |  | r | rʲ |  |  |

- There is scholarly disagreement over whether palatalization was phonemic in Proto-Romance.
- Palatalized consonants tended to geminate between vowels. The extent of this varied by consonant.
- /tʲ/ would have been an affricate like [t͡sʲ] or [t͡zʲ] .

- /sC/ in word-initial position was assigned a prop-vowel , as in /ˈstare/ .
- /ɡn/ was likely at first, with later developments varying by region.
- /d ɡ/ might have been fricatives or approximants between vowels.
- /ll/ might have been retroflex .
- /f/ might have been bilabial .

- /b/ did not occur in intervocalic position.

## Morphology

The forms below are spelt as they are in the cited sources, either in Latin style or in phonetic notation. The latter may not always agree with the phonology given above.

### Nouns

Nouns are reconstructed as having three cases: a nominative, an accusative, and a genitive - dative:

| Type |  | -a (f) |  | -o (m) |  | -C (m) |  | -C (f) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Number | SG | PL | SG | PL | SG | PL | SG | PL |
| NOM | capra | capras | caballus | caballi | frater | fratres/-i | noctis | noctes |
| ACC | caballu | caballos | fratre | fratres | nocte |
| GEN-DAT | caprae | capris | caballo | caballis | fratri | fratris | nocti | noctis |
| Gloss | ‘goat’ | ‘horse’ | ‘brother’ | ‘night’ |

Some nouns of the –C type had inflections with alternating stress or syllable count:

| Type |  | -C (m) |  | -C (f) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Number | SG | PL | SG | PL |
| NOM | hómo | hómines/-i | múlier | muliéres |
| ACC | hómine | hómines | muliére |
| GEN-DAT | hómini | hóminis | muliéri | muliéris |
| Gloss | ‘man’ | ‘woman’ |

There were also ‘neuter’ nouns. In the singular they would have been treated as masculine and in the plural as feminine, often with a collective sense.

| Type |  | -o (n) |  | -C (n) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Number | SG | PL | SG | PL |
| NOM | bracchiu | bracchia | corpus | corpora |
| ACC |
| GEN-DAT | bracchio | bracchiis | corpori | corporis |
| Gloss | ‘arm’ | ‘body’ |

### Adjectives

| Type |  | -o/-a |  |  |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Gender | M |  | F | M |  | F |
| Number | SG | PL | SG | PL | SG | PL | SG | PL |
| NOM | bonus | boni | bona | bonas | virdis | virdes/-i | virdis | virdes |
| ACC | bonu | bonos | virde | virdes | virde |
| GEN-DAT | bono | bonis | bonae | bonis | virdi | virdis | virdi | virdis |
| Gloss | ‘good’ | ‘green’ |

For the most part, the typical way to form a comparative would have been to add magis or plus (‘more’) to a positive adjective. A few words can be reconstructed as having a comparative ending -ior, which would have been inflected as follows:

| Number |  | SG |
|---|---|---|
| Gender | M or F | N |
| NOM | mélior | mélius |
| ACC | melióre |
| Gloss | ‘better’ |

Superlatives would have been formed by adding definite articles to comparatives.

### Pronouns

The stressed or 'strong' forms:

| Person |  | 1 |  | 2 |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Number | SG | PL | SG | PL |
| NOM | ego | nos | tu | vos |
| ACC | me/mene | te/tene |
| DAT | mi/mibi | nobis | ti/tibi | vobis |

| Person |  | 3 (m) |  | 3 (f) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Number | SG | PL | SG | PL |
| NOM | ille/illi/ipse | illi/ipsi | illa/ipsa | illas/ipsas |
| ACC | illu/ipsu | illos/ipsos |
| (GEN-)DAT | illui/ipsui | illoru/ipsoru | illaei/ipsaei | illoru/ipsoru |

The unstressed or 'weak' forms:

| Person |  | 1 |  | 2 |  | 3 (m) |  | 3 (f) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Number | SG | PL | SG | PL | SG | PL | SG | PL |
| ACC | me | nos | te | vos | lu | los | la | las |
| DAT | mi | tī | li | lis | li | lis |

As follows:

| Gender |  | M or F | N |
|---|---|---|---|
| NOM | qui | quid (/quod?) |
| ACC | quem |
| DAT | cui | – |

(/quod?)

### Verbs

| Verb class |  | 1P |  | 2P |  | 3P |  | Infinitive |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| SG | PL | SG | PL | SG | PL |
| I | kánto | kantámųs | kántas | kantátįs | kántat | kántant | kantáre |
| IIa | dǫ́rm(j)o | dormímųs | dǫ́rmįs | dormítįs | dǫ́rmįt | dǫ́rmųnt/-ent | dormíre |
| IIb | florésko/-í- | florímųs | floréskįs/-í- | florítįs | floréskįt/-í- | floréskųnt/-í- | floríre |
| IIIa | wį́dd’o | wįdémųs | wį́des | wįdétįs | wį́det | wį́dųnt/-ent (wį́dd’ųnt) | wįdére |

<div class="sidebar-list mw-collapsible mw-collapsed mw-made-collapsible"><button type="button" class="mw-collapsible-toggle mw-collapsible-toggle-default mw-collapsible-toggle-collapsed" aria-expanded="false" tabindex="0"><span class="mw-collapsible-text">show</span></button><div class="sidebar-list-title" style="border-top:1px solid #aaa; text-align:center;background-color:#e0e0e0;;color: var(--color-base)"><a href="/wiki/History_of_artificial_intelligence" title="History of artificial intelligence">History</a></div><div class="sidebar-list-content mw-collapsible-content" hidden="until-found">

<li><a href="/wiki/AI_bubble" title="AI bubble">AI bubble</a></li></ul></div></div></td>
</tr><tr><td class="sidebar-content">
<div class="sidebar-list mw-collapsible mw-collapsed mw-made-collapsible"><button type="button" class="mw-collapsible-toggle mw-collapsible-toggle-default mw-collapsible-toggle-collapsed" aria-expanded="false" tabindex="0"><span class="mw-collapsible-text">show</span></button><div class="sidebar-list-title" style="border-top:1px solid #aaa; text-align:center;background-color:#e0e0e0;;color: var(--color-base)"><a href="/wiki/Artificial_intelligence_controversies" title="Artificial intelligence controversies">Controversies</a></div><div class="sidebar-list-content mw-collapsible-content" hidden="until-found">

<li><a href="/wiki/Voiceverse_NFT_plagiarism_scandal" title="Voiceverse NFT plagiarism scandal">Voiceverse NFT plagiarism scandal</a></li></ul></div></div></td>
</tr><tr><td class="sidebar-content">
<div class="sidebar-list mw-collapsible mw-collapsed mw-made-collapsible"><button type="button" class="mw-collapsible-toggle mw-collapsible-toggle-default mw-collapsible-toggle-collapsed" aria-expanded="false" tabindex="0"><span class="mw-collapsible-text">show</span></button><div class="sidebar-list-title" style="border-top:1px solid #aaa; text-align:center;background-color:#e0e0e0;;color: var(--color-base)">Glossary</div><div class="sidebar-list-content mw-collapsible-content" hidden="until-found">
<ul><li><a href="/wiki/Glossary_of_artificial_intelligence" title="Glossary of artificial intelligence">Glossary</a></li></ul></div></div></td>
</tr><tr><td class="sidebar-navbar"><link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1333133064"><style data-mw-deduplicate="TemplateStyles:r1239400231">.mw-parser-output .navbar{display:inline;font-size:88%;font-weight:normal}.mw-parser-output .navbar-collapse{float:left;text-align:left}.mw-parser-output .navbar-boxtext{word-spacing:0}.mw-parser-output .navbar ul{display:inline-block;white-space:nowrap;line-height:inherit}.mw-parser-output .navbar-brackets::before{margin-right:-0.125em;content:"[ "}.mw-parser-output .navbar-brackets::after{margin-left:-0.125em;content:" ]"}.mw-parser-output .navbar li{word-spacing:-0.125em}.mw-parser-output .navbar a>span,.mw-parser-output .navbar a>abbr{text-decoration:inherit}.mw-parser-output .navbar-mini abbr{font-variant:small-caps;border-bottom:none;text-decoration:none;cursor:inherit}.mw-parser-output .navbar-ct-full{font-size:114%;margin:0 7em}.mw-parser-output .navbar-ct-mini{font-size:114%;margin:0 4em}html.skin-theme-clientpref-night .mw-parser-output .navbar li a abbr{color:var(--color-base)!important}@media(prefers-color-scheme:dark){html.skin-theme-clientpref-os .mw-parser-output .navbar li a abbr{color:var(--color-base)!important}}@media print{.mw-parser-output .navbar{display:none!important}}</style><div class="navbar plainlinks hlist navbar-mini"><ul><li class="nv-view"><a href="/wiki/Template:Artificial_intelligence" title="Template:Artificial intelligence"><abbr title="View this template">v</abbr></a></li><li class="nv-talk"><a href="/wiki/Template_talk:Artificial_intelligence" title="Template talk:Artificial intelligence"><abbr title="Discuss this template">t</abbr></a></li><li class="nv-edit"><a href="/wiki/Special:EditPage/Template:Artificial_intelligence" title="Special:EditPage/Template:Artificial intelligence"><abbr title="Edit this template">e</abbr></a></li></ul></div></td></tr></tbody></table>
<p><b>Artificial intelligence</b> (<b>AI</b>) is the capability of <a href="/wiki/Computer" title="Computer">computational systems</a> to perform tasks typically associated with <a href="/wiki/Human_intelligence" title="Human intelligence">human intelligence</a>, such as <a href="/wiki/Learning" title="Learning">learning</a>, <a href="/wiki/Reason" title="Reason">reasoning</a>, <a href="/wiki/Problem-solving" class="mw-redirect" title="Problem-solving">problem-solving</a>, <a href="/wiki/Perception" title="Perception">perception</a>, and <a href="/wiki/Decision-making" title="Decision-making">decision-making</a>. It is a <a href="/wiki/Field_of_research" class="mw-redirect" title="Field of research">field of research</a> in <a href="/wiki/Engineering" title="Engineering">engineering</a>, <a href="/wiki/Mathematics" title="Mathematics">mathematics</a> and <a href="/wiki/Computer_science" title="Computer science">computer science</a> that develops and studies methods and <a href="/wiki/Software" title="Software">software</a> that enable machines to perceive their environment and use <a href="/wiki/Machine_learning" title="Machine learning">learning</a> and <a href="/wiki/Intelligence" title="Intelligence">intelligence</a> to take actions that maximize their chances of achieving defined goals.<sup id="cite_ref-FOOTNOTERussellNorvig20211–4_1-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-FOOTNOTERussellNorvig20211–4-1"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>1<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup>
</p><p>High-profile <a href="/wiki/Applications_of_artificial_intelligence" title="Applications of artificial intelligence">applications of AI</a> include advanced <a href="/wiki/Web_search_engine" class="mw-redirect" title="Web search engine">web search engines</a>, <a href="/wiki/Chatbot" title="Chatbot">chatbots</a>, <a href="/wiki/Virtual_assistant" title="Virtual assistant">virtual assistants</a>, <a href="/wiki/Autonomous_vehicles" class="mw-redirect" title="Autonomous vehicles">autonomous vehicles</a>, and play and analysis in <a href="/wiki/Strategy_game" title="Strategy game">strategy games</a> (e.g., <a href="/wiki/Chess" title="Chess">chess</a> and <a href="/wiki/Go_(game)" title="Go (game)">Go</a>). Since the 2020s, <a href="/wiki/Generative_AI" title="Generative AI">generative AI</a> has become widely available to generate images, audio, and videos from text prompts.
</p><p>The traditional goals of AI research include learning, <a href="/wiki/Automated_reasoning" title="Automated reasoning">reasoning</a>, <a href="/wiki/Knowledge_representation" class="mw-redirect" title="Knowledge representation">knowledge representation</a>, <a href="/wiki/Automated_planning_and_scheduling" title="Automated planning and scheduling">planning</a>, <a href="/wiki/Natural_language_processing" title="Natural language processing">natural language processing</a>, and <a href="/wiki/Machine_perception" title="Machine perception">perception</a>, as well as support for <a href="/wiki/Robotics" title="Robotics">robotics</a>.<sup id="cite_ref-Problems_of_AI_2-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Problems_of_AI-2"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>a<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> To reach these goals, AI researchers have used techniques including <a href="/wiki/State_space_search" class="mw-redirect" title="State space search">state space search</a> and <a href="/wiki/Mathematical_optimization" title="Mathematical optimization">mathematical optimization</a>, <a href="/wiki/Formal_logic" class="mw-redirect" title="Formal logic">formal logic</a>, <a href="/wiki/Artificial_neural_network" class="mw-redirect" title="Artificial neural network">artificial neural networks</a>, and methods based on <a href="/wiki/Statistics" title="Statistics">statistics</a>, <a href="/wiki/Operations_research" title="Operations research">operations research</a>, and <a href="/wiki/Economics" title="Economics">economics</a>.<sup id="cite_ref-Tools_of_AI_3-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Tools_of_AI-3"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>b<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> AI also draws upon <a href="/wiki/Psychology" title="Psychology">psychology</a>, <a href="/wiki/Linguistics" title="Linguistics">linguistics</a>, <a href="/wiki/Philosophy_of_artificial_intelligence" title="Philosophy of artificial intelligence">philosophy</a>, <a href="/wiki/Neuroscience" title="Neuroscience">neuroscience</a>, and other fields.<sup id="cite_ref-4" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-4"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>2<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> Some companies, such as <a href="/wiki/OpenAI" title="OpenAI">OpenAI</a>, <a href="/wiki/Google_DeepMind" title="Google DeepMind">Google DeepMind</a> and <a href="/wiki/Meta_Platforms" title="Meta Platforms">Meta</a>, aim to create <a href="/wiki/Artificial_general_intelligence" title="Artificial general intelligence">artificial general intelligence</a> (AGI) – AI that can complete virtually any cognitive task at least as well as a
